query: oral glucose tolerance test cut off values

retrieved_docs.title: Hexokinase Method -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Criteria for diagnosis of DM have been defined and updated from time to time by the American Diabetes Association (ADA). Diagnosis of DM is based on the cut-off values for plasma glucose levels in fasting, 2 hours post 75 g glucose load (oral glucose tolerance test - OGTT), and values of HbA1c fraction of glycated hemoglobin. [32] [33] The table shows the cut-off values prescribed by ADA for diagnosing DM.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Glucose -- Related Testing -- Oral Glucose Tolerance Test
retrieved_docs.content: Impaired glucose tolerance: Impaired glucose tolerance  is characterized by elevated blood glucose levels during the oral glucose tolerance test, exceeding normal thresholds but not meeting the diagnostic criteria for diabetes mellitus. This condition represents an intermediate stage of dysglycemia, placing individuals at risk of developing diabetes mellitus and cardiovascular complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Glucose Tolerance Test -- Testing Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Serial measurement of plasma glucose before and after a specific oral glucose load provides a standard method to evaluate individuals and establish values for healthy and diseased subjects. Although more sensitive than FPG determinations, GTT is influenced by multiple factors, resulting in poor reproducibility. [30] Approximately 20% of OGTTs fall into the nondiagnostic category (eg, only 1 blood sample exhibits increased glucose concentration). Unless results are grossly abnormal initially, the OGTT should be performed on 2 separate occasions to establish a diabetes diagnosis. Conditions for performing an OGTT include the following:

retrieved_docs.title: Plasma Glucose -- Testing Procedures -- Oral Glucose Tolerance Test (OGTT)
retrieved_docs.content: Serial measurement of plasma glucose before and after a specific amount of glucose should provide a standard method to evaluate individuals and establish values for healthy and diseased subjects. [56] Although more sensitive than fasting plasma glucose determinations, glucose tolerance testing is affected by multiple factors that result in poor reproducibility. [57] Unless results are grossly abnormal initially, the OGTT should be performed on 2 separate occasions to establish the diagnosis of DM. [58]

retrieved_docs.title: Glucose Intolerance -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The American Diabetes Association (ADA) states that the risk of developing diabetes is continuous and extends below the lower limit of the range while becoming disproportionately greater at the upper limit of the range of for 3 diagnostic tests: Fasting plasma glucose The 75-gm oral glucose tolerance test (GTT, 2-hour value) Glycated hemoglobin (A1C) [1] .

retrieved_docs.title: Glucose Tolerance Test -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: IFG is defined as having FPG levels of 100 to 125 mg/dL (5.6 to 6.9 mmol/L). IGT is characterized by having 2-hour postglucose load levels ranging from 140 to 199 mg/dL (7.8 to 11.0 mmol/L) during a 75-gram OGTT. [34] The World Health Organization and several other diabetes organizations define the lower IFG limit as 110 mg/dL (6.1 mmol/L). [35] The results of the OGTT as a screening test for T2DM may be interpreted as follows: 2-hr plasma glucose below 140 mg/dL: normal 2-hr plasma glucose ranging from 140 to 199 mg/dL: IGT 2-hr plasma glucose of at least 200 mg/dL: diabetes

retrieved_docs.title: Glucose Tolerance Test -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings -- Two-Step Strategy for Screening and Diagnosis of G estational Diabetes Mellitus
retrieved_docs.content: A 100-gram OGTT is recommended if plasma glucose at 1 hour after a 50-g glucose load, performed at 24 to 28 weeks of gestation in those not previously diagnosed with diabetes, reaches or exceeds threshold values of 130, 135, or 140 mg/dL (7.2, 7.5, or 7.8 mmol/L, respectively). The assessment should be conducted when the patient is fasting. A diagnosis of GDM is established if at least 2 out of 4 plasma glucose measurements taken fasting and at 1, 2, and 3 hours during the OGTT meet or exceed the following. FPG: 95 mg/dL (5.3 mmol/L) 1-hr plasma glucose: 180 mg/dL (10.0 mmol/L) 2-hr plasma glucose: 155 mg/dL (8.6 mmol/L) 3-hr plasma glucose: 140 mg/dL (7.8 mmol/L)

retrieved_docs.title: Plasma Glucose -- Testing Procedures -- Oral Glucose Tolerance Test (OGTT)
retrieved_docs.content: The following conditions should be met before an OGTT is performed: discontinue, when possible, medications known to affect glucose tolerance; perform in the morning after 3 days of unrestricted diet (containing at least 150 g of carbohydrate per day) and activity; and perform the test after a 10- to 16-hour fast only in ambulatory outpatients (bed rest impairs glucose tolerance). The patient should remain seated during the test. [56]

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetes -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Fasting glucose levels and HbA1c testing are useful for the early identification of T2DM. If borderline, a glucose tolerance test is an option to evaluate both fasting glucose levels and serum response to an oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT). Prediabetes, which often precedes T2DM, presents with a fasting blood glucose level of 100 to 125 mg/dL or a 2-hour post-oral glucose tolerance test (post-OGTT) glucose level of 140 to 200 mg/dL. [24] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Glucose -- Related Testing -- Oral Glucose Tolerance Test
retrieved_docs.content: Normal response: In individuals with normal glucose tolerance, blood glucose levels rise temporarily following glucose ingestion but return to fasting levels within 2 hours. Normal oral glucose tolerance test results indicate efficient glucose uptake and insulin secretion, indicative of intact glucose metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Glucose -- Related Testing -- Oral Glucose Tolerance Test
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes mellitus is diagnosed when the oral glucose tolerance test reveals sustained hyperglycemia, with fasting glucose ≥126 mg/dL (7.0 mmol/L) or 2-hour post-load glucose levels ≥200 mg/dL (11.1 mmol/L) on 2 separate occasions. The oral glucose tolerance test is particularly useful for diagnosing gestational diabetes mellitus, a transient form of diabetes mellitus occurring during pregnancy. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Plasma Glucose -- Testing Procedures -- Oral Glucose Tolerance Test (OGTT)
retrieved_docs.content: Glucose tolerance testing should not be performed on hospitalized, acutely ill, or inactive patients. The test should begin between 7:00 am and 9:00 am. Venous plasma glucose should be measured while fasting and 2 hours after an oral glucose load. For nonpregnant adults, the recommended load is 75 g, which may not be a maximum stimulus; 15 g for children, 1.75 g/kg, up to 75 g maximum, is given. The glucose should be dissolved in 300 mL of water and ingested over 5 minutes. [58]

retrieved_docs.title: Glucose Tolerance Test -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Confirming a diagnosis requires repeating the test on another day shortly afterward and yielding similar results. Alternatively, a diagnosis may be established using one of the other recommended screening tests. A single abnormal OGTT is insufficient to diagnose diabetes or prediabetes. The American Diabetes Association recommends either the 1- or 2-step approach at 24 to 28 weeks of gestation for pregnant patients not previously known to have diabetes. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Glucose Tolerance Test -- Diagnostic Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Fasting plasma glucose (FPG), glycated hemoglobin A1C (HbA1C), and 2-hour post-glucose load measurement during a 75-gram OGTT are all suitable for diabetes diagnostic screening. [29] Detection rates of different screening tests vary across populations and individuals. The efficacy of interventions for primary prevention of T2DM has mainly been demonstrated in people with impaired glucose tolerance (IGT), with or without elevated fasting glucose, rather than in individuals with isolated impaired fasting glucose (IFG) or prediabetes according to HbA1C thresholds.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Glucose -- Related Testing -- Oral Glucose Tolerance Test
retrieved_docs.content: This test is valuable in assessing glucose metabolism, offering insights into the body's response to ingested glucose. The test aids in the early detection of diabetes and pre-diabetes mellitus, enabling timely interventions to prevent or delay disease progression and associated complications. The test involves administering a standardized oral glucose load, typically 75 g, after an overnight fast, followed by serial blood glucose measurements at specified intervals, typically 0, 30, 60, 90, and 120 minutes post-glucose ingestion. Blood samples are collected to assess glucose concentrations, reflecting the body's ability to handle a glucose challenge. [24] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2310
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin aspart, human, a rapid‑acting insulin analog, is indicated for the management of both مرض السكري نوع 1 and مرض السكري نوع 2. In patients with مرض السكري نوع 1, insulin aspart replaces the absent endogenous insulin to achieve glycemic control, while in مرض السكري نوع 2 it is used when oral agents are insufficient or when insulin therapy is required to maintain target blood glucose levels. Its use in both forms of diabetes underscores the central role of insulin replacement in achieving optimal metabolic control across the diabetes spectrum.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2479
retrieved_docs.content: Disease condition determination, uncontrolled is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, indicating that an uncontrolled disease state is linked to the presence of diabetes mellitus that has progressed to a complicated form, underscoring the importance of effective disease management to prevent or mitigate diabetic complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1267
retrieved_docs.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes several specific pancreatic endocrine disorders. Among its children are ارتفاع السكر في الدم, which describes the elevated blood‑glucose levels that characterize hyperglycemia; مرض السكري دون مضاعفات, which refers to diabetes that has not yet produced secondary complications; فرط أنسولين الدم, indicating an excess of circulating insulin often seen in early insulin‑resistance states or insulin‑producing tumors; and ورم غلوكاغوني, a rare glucagon‑secreting tumor (glucagonoma) that can also cause hyperglycemia. These conditions are interrelated through their impact on pancreatic hormone production and regulation of blood glucose, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of pancreatic endocrine function in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2267
retrieved_docs.content: Topiramat is classified as a hypoglykemika, meaning it belongs to the class of agents that lower blood glucose levels. Recognizing topiramat as a hypoglycemic medication is important for understanding its potential impact on glucose metabolism and for guiding appropriate patient monitoring and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1478
retrieved_docs.content: Glibenklamid is identified as a member of the class of Sulfonylureas for blood glucose lowering, indicating that it belongs to this group of oral antidiabetic agents that reduce plasma glucose levels by stimulating insulin secretion from the pancreas. As a sulfonylurea, glibenklamid plays a role in therapeutic strategies for managing hyperglycemia in patients with type 2 diabetes, underscoring its importance in achieving effective glycemic control.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1621
retrieved_docs.content: Glucose 100 mg/mL injection is formulated with dextrose as its pharmacologically active component; anhydrous dextrose constitutes the active moiety of the preparation, while dextrose monohydrate serves as the active ingredient. Both forms of dextrose provide the therapeutic glucose delivered by the injection, ensuring consistent dosing and efficacy in clinical use.

retrieved_docs.content: Metformin is classified as a member of the biguanide blood‑glucose‑lowering drug class, which comprises agents used to reduce blood glucose levels.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 202

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2157
retrieved_docs.content: Increased plasma osmolality is clinically associated with elevated blood glucose levels (hyperglycemia). This rise in plasma osmolality often originates from dehydration, as fluid loss concentrates solutes in the bloodstream. Additionally, increased plasma osmolality commonly co‑occurs with low blood volume (hypovolemia). Recognizing that dehydration can lead to higher plasma osmolality, which in turn may signal hyperglycemia and accompany hypovolemia, is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate fluid and glucose management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2075
retrieved_docs.content: Glibenklamid and glimepiride are both members of the sulfonylurea class of oral agents used for blood glucose lowering. As sulfonylureas, they act by stimulating pancreatic insulin secretion, thereby helping to control hyperglycemia in patients with diabetes mellitus. Recognizing that glibenklamid and glimepiride belong to the same therapeutic group informs clinicians about their similar mechanisms of action, potential side effect profiles, and considerations for interchangeable use in diabetes management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2372
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance that arises during pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is recognized as a specific type of complication within the broader category of complications of pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium. This condition represents a distinct obstetric complication where altered glucose metabolism adversely affects maternal and fetal health, underscoring the importance of monitoring and managing glucose levels throughout gestation, delivery, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 569
retrieved_docs.content: Anhydrous dextrose serves as the active moiety of the medication glucose 100 MG/ML Injection, meaning it is the pharmacologically effective component of this injectable glucose solution used to provide rapid carbohydrate supplementation in clinical settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 778
retrieved_docs.content: Sulfonylureas for blood glucose lowering is a therapeutic class that includes the agents glibenclamide, glimepiride, and tolbutamide. These sulfonylurea drugs lower blood glucose by stimulating pancreatic insulin secretion and are commonly employed in the management of type 2 diabetes mellitus. In addition to its role as a glucose‑lowering medication, tolbutamide is also listed as a member of diagnostic agents for diabetes testing, where it can be used in oral glucose tolerance tests to assess pancreatic beta‑cell function. The inclusion of glibenclamide, glimepiride, and tolbutamide within the sulfonylurea class highlights their shared mechanism of action and therapeutic purpose, while tolbutamide’s dual classification underscores its utility both as a treatment agent and as a diagnostic tool in diabetes care.
